---
title: "Week 3: Fundamentals of questionnaire design"
subtitle: "CHC9012 | National Taiwan Normal University"
editor: source
---

## Overview

This week, we will cover the basics of questionnaire (or *experimental*) design. We will learn the technical terms to describe the methodology employed in a questionnaire or experiment, which will be essential for you when reading academic papers, attending conferences, and writing your own final term papers.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## 🧪 Core concepts
**Experimental design variables & structure**

We will discuss the following core concepts through several studies involving language:

- **Variables:** Independent variable(s) and dependent variable(s).
- **Types of design:** Within-participants design, between-participants design, and mixed designs.
- **Factorial design:** What “factors” and “levels” actually mean.
- **Counterbalancing:** Why there is a need to counterbalance, and how Latin square designs work to achieve it.

## 📚 Required readings
**Discussion material for this week**

Our discussion of these concepts will be based on the following two readings:

- **Abbuhl et al. (2014)** *(from the book "Research Methods in Linguistics")*
- **Rogers & Révész (2020)** *(from the book "The Routledge Handbook of Research Methods in Applied Linguistics")*

:::

<br>

### 📊 Graphic Summary

Here is a visual breakdown to help you synthesize the experimental design concepts we are discussing this week:

![](Week3_Summary.svg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

<hr>

### 📖 Concept definitions & examples

| Concept | Definition | Example application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Independent variable** | The variable that is manipulated by the researcher, also known as the variable of influence. It is expected to bring about variation or change in the dependent variable. | Corrective feedback |
| **Dependent variable** | The variable that is measured or influenced. | Grammatical development |
| **Between-participants design** | A design where each level of the independent variable is comprised of different participants. | Typically developing (TD) children and children with specific language impairment (SLI) |
| **Within-participants design** | A design where the same individuals are assigned to all levels of the independent variable. | A class studying half of target items under a spaced-short condition and the other half under a spaced-long condition |
| **Mixed design** | A design that combines both within-participants and between-participants factors. | Examining L2 performance using task complexity (within-participants) and modality (between-participants) |
| **Factorial design** | A research design that includes more than one independent variable to examine separate and combined effects. | Investigating the effects of wh-dependency and dependency length on sentence processing using a 2 x 2 design |
| **Counterbalancing** | Having different, randomly assigned participants complete the different levels of the independent variable in different orders to address order or carryover effects. A Latin-square design is frequently used for this purpose to control against test- and task-order effects. | Counterbalancing four different tasks across four groups in a Latin-square design so each group completes tasks in a different order |

<hr>

::: {.callout-important icon="true"}
## 📝 Assignment for next week

Please complete the following tasks before our next meeting:

1. **Brainstorm:** Review the concepts we’ve been through today. Start thinking of your own research question and how to translate it using the variables and design structures we've learned this week.
2. **Read:** Prepare the materials for Week 4:
   - *Schütze & Sprouse (2014)*
   - *Kaiser (2014)*
:::
